text: This is a case of myolipoma of the right adrenal gland.
The patient is positioned semi-laterally, and the surgeon inserts a fan retractor through the epigastric port to facilitate exposure of the surgical field.
The right lobe of the liver is visible, with the myolipoma of the right adrenal gland positioned in front of it.
The surgeon uses a hook to open the peritoneum on the right side, near the gallbladder, revealing underlying structures.
The myolipoma is visibly adhered to the inferior surface of the right lobe of the liver, with the surrounding anatomical structures clearly exposed.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to carefully separate the peritoneum and detach the myolipoma from the inferior surface of the right lobe of the liver.
The surgeon observes a large myolipoma, approximately fifteen centimeters in size, visibly pressing against the right kidney.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers instruments near the metal part of the fan detector, ensuring it does not make direct contact to prevent coupling.
The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to stretch the peritoneum, aiding in the separation of the myolipoma from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon exposes the capsule of the myolipoma, which is visible beneath the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully separates the outer covering of the pituitary gland, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon employs a combination of blunt dissection and hook dissection techniques to manipulate and separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies a good dissection plane, revealing the areolar tissue as the surgical field is exposed.
The surgeon addresses minor bleeding from the surface of the liver, using coagulation to control the bleeding before cutting the tissue.

The surgeon stretches the peritoneum to expose the underlying vena cava, while carefully identifying and avoiding the duodenum. The peritoneum is separated to facilitate the medialization of the duodenum.
The surgeon identifies the lower edge of the surgical field, revealing a portion of the kidney and the upper pole of the kidney.
The surgeon identifies a vein in the surgical field, with the vena cava visible below it, highlighting the spatial relationship between these vascular structures.
The surgical field shows the anatomical structures near the vena cava, with the tissue almost touching the vena cava.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to perform blunt dissection, clearing the surgical field and separating tissue layers.
The renal vein is visible, branching from the vena cava, with clear anatomical delineation between these structures.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein inferiorly and uses a Maryland dissector to create a window around the vein, preparing it for clipping.
The surgeon has skeletonized the vein, revealing its structure and surrounding tissue, with the vena cava visible below.
The surgeon uses a hook to detach the adrenal vein from the myolipoma, creating a window around the vein. A titanium clip is then applied to secure the vein.

An extra clip is removed using a disposable clip applicator. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the inferior surface of the adrenal gland, while a fan retractor is placed to optimize traction. The remaining attachments to the liver are carefully separated with the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon performs superior mobilization using blunt dissection, creating a clear aerial plane that separates the lipoma from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the adrenal gland inferiorly from the superior pole of the kidney, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the gland from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon separates the tissue inferiorly, identifying a clear dissection plane to facilitate dorsal mobilization of the adrenal gland.
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to cut and separate the remaining attachments of the adrenal gland's inferior surface from the surrounding muscles, completing the mobilization of this area.
The assistant holds the suction device to manage any oozing from the bed of the lipoma, ensuring a clear surgical field. Minimal bleeding is observed from the lipoma bed.
The surgeon lifts the inferior pole of the lipoma, applying blunt traction to detach it from surrounding tissues, visibly achieving near-complete separation.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal gland located lateral to the vena cava, and begins to separate it from its attachments. The superior attachments are visible and will be carefully dissected.
The surgeon identifies small attachments to the lateral wall, consisting of peritoneal tags, and uses a harmonic scalpel to cut and separate them.
The assistant applies traction to inspect the bed of the large lipoma, which is breaking slightly due to the traction from suction irrigation.
The surgeon frees all pectoral attachments and gently lifts the lipoma, revealing the superior pole of the kidney below, which is visibly separated from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon completes the blunt dissection, freeing the lipoma completely from its attachments superiorly and medially. The lipoma flips upward, revealing collected blood in the surgical field. The harmonic scalpel is used to separate the blood and control any bleeding.
The surgeon completes the dorsal mobilization of the adrenal gland, ensuring it is fully separated from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection to separate the remaining attachments, revealing the kidney below. The myolipoma is placed into an endo bag, which is then extracted through the incision for mechanical morcellation.

The surgeon identifies a small superior attachment of the myolipoma to the peritoneum and performs suction irrigation to check for any bleeding. The attachment is carefully divided using a harmonic scalpel, and the myolipoma is placed into a large endo bag for removal.
The surgeon places the myolipoma inside the endo bag, extrudes the mouth of the bag, and performs manual morcellation using ovum forceps to remove the tissue.
